The World of Competitive Scrabble

A look inside the strategy, vocabulary, and competitive fire behind every tile rack.

My Scrabble Journey

I've been playing competitive Scrabble since I was a teenager. What started as a casual family game turned into a deep passion for word strategy, board control, and the thrill of tournament play. I've attended three national championships and countless local tournaments, each one sharpening my skills and expanding my word knowledge.

Competitive Scrabble is nothing like the game most people know from their living rooms. Tournament players memorize thousands of obscure two- and three-letter words, study tile distribution probabilities, and think several moves ahead—much like chess, but with letters.

Top Strategies for Competitive Play

  1. Master the Two-Letter Words
    • There are over 100 valid two-letter words in tournament Scrabble
    • Words like QI, ZA, and XI unlock high-scoring parallel plays
    • Knowing every two-letter word lets you play almost anywhere on the board
  2. Control the Board
    • Avoid opening triple-word-score lanes for your opponent
    • Play tight when you're ahead, open the board when you're behind
    • Block premium squares strategically rather than chasing them yourself
  3. Track Your Tiles
    • Keep a mental (or physical) count of which tiles have been played
    • In the endgame, knowing your opponent's remaining tiles is decisive
    • Tile tracking helps you decide when to exchange versus when to play
  4. Learn the Bingo Stems
    • SATINE, RETINA, and other common seven-letter combinations
    • A "bingo" (using all 7 tiles) earns a 50-point bonus
    • Drilling anagram exercises daily builds bingo-finding speed

Watch Competitive Scrabble in Action

This video from the 2019 North American Scrabble Championship shows the intensity and strategy that define top-level play.

Scrabble by the Numbers

This interactive Tableau visualization explores popular board games and their ratings. Filter and hover to discover how Scrabble stacks up against other classics.
